items.title: Pope made us feel that writers are useful says Igiaba Scego
items.description: (ANSA) - ROME, JUN 24 - Pope Leo XIV made an international delegation of authors feel that writers are useful during an audience Wednesday marking the 100th anniversary of the founding of Vatican publisher Libreria Editrice Vataicnaa (LEV), Italo-Somali novelist Igiaba Scego told ANSA after their encounter with the American pontiff. "Pope Leo made us feel that writers are very useful. Fiction is often marginalized, and yet, precisely because we live in a world of artificial intelligence and unbridled capitalism, understanding that storytelling is the center of life was important. The Pope's wo
items.titleIt: Pope made us feel that writers are useful says Igiaba Scego

items.description: The decree prohibits citizens of Sudan, Eritrea, Ethiopia, and Somalia from entering through all land, sea, and air ports.

items.title: PM's office asks 2 mn euro Regeni compensation
items.description: (ANSA) - ROME, JUN 24 - Premier Giorgia Meloni's office on Wednesday requested compensation of two million euros from four Egyptian national security officials charged with abducting and torturing to death Italian student Giulio Regeni in Cairo in January-February 2016. Speaking at their in-absentia trial in Rome, the State Attorney said that "this crime has profoundly affected the Italian community" and that the defendants "have violated the rights to the protection of Italian citizens' freedoms, even abroad. "Constitutionally guaranteed rights have been violated." The 28-year-old student's h
items.titleIt: PM's office asks 2 mn euro Regeni compensation

items.title: “Strategic Balance” under Stress: Egypt and the U.S.-Israeli War with Iran
items.description: Egypt has been walking a tightrope since the U.S.-Israeli war with Iran began, joining mediation efforts while signalling solidarity with Gulf Arab partners under Iranian attack. But with some Gulf capitals voicing expectations of stronger support, Cairo may find itself under increasing pressure.
items.titleIt: “Strategic Balance” under Stress: Egypt and the U.S.-Israeli War with Iran

items.title: Sudan, South Sudan and Somalia: Key Peace and Security Priorities for Africa in 2026
items.description: In this online event, Crisis Group experts discussed peace and security priorities for the African continent in 2026, zooming in on Somalia, Sudan and South Sudan ahead of the African Union's summit of heads of state.

items.title: Africa: Inside the Floating Lab Shaping Ocean Science
items.description: [allAfrica] The first thing you notice is the size of the Dr. Fridtjof Nansen. It towers over the quay as it is docked at the Port of Mombasa under the fluttering Kenyan flag.

items.title: Ex Berlusconi villa in Sardinia sold to Qatari sheikh, ex PM
items.description: (ANSA) - ROME, JUN 24 - The former Sardinian luxury villa of late premier Silvio Berlusconi has been sold to Qatari Sheikh Hamad bin Jassim bin Jaber Al Thani, the former prime minister of the Gulf emirate, the Berlusconi family holding company Fininvest said Wednesday, confirming media reports. Villa Certosa has been sold to the royal family's Constellation real estate group for an undisclosed sum, it said. The Sultan of Brunei had also voiced interest in the 500 million euro villa, for decades one of the most famous private estates in Europe. Located on the stunning coastline of Sardinia, th
items.titleIt: Ex Berlusconi villa in Sardinia sold to Qatari sheikh, ex PM

items.title: Rwanda: Parliament Clears Rwf207bn for Disaster Response
items.description: [New Times] The Chamber of Deputies on Wednesday, June 24, approved a draft law authorising Rwanda to ratify a $141 million (about Rwf207 billion) loan agreement with the International Development Association (IDA) to support disaster risk management through a contingent emergency response mechanism.
items.titleIt: Rwanda: Parliament Clears Rwf207bn for Disaster Response

items.title: EU countries give 1st green light to aid for soaring fertiliser prices
items.description: (ANSA) - ROME, JUN 25 - EU member states have given rapid approval to support measures aimed at helping farmers cope with rising fertiliser costs driven by the conflict in the Middle East. The proposal for targeted amendments to the Common Agricultural Policy (CAP), presented last week by the European Commission, will now enter negotiations with the European Parliament. Under the current 2021-2027 CAP framework, Brussels has proposed a temporary liquidity scheme, the possibility for member states to advance direct payments at a higher rate in 2026, and greater flexibility to reprogramme funds
items.titleIt: EU countries give 1st green light to aid for soaring fertiliser prices

items.title: European Parliament activates urgent procedure for fertiliser price support measures
items.description: (ANSA) - ROME, JUN 25 - The European Parliament has activated the urgent procedure to approve support measures for farmers affected by soaring fertiliser prices linked to the conflict in the Middle East. The proposal for targeted amendments to the Common Agricultural Policy was presented by the European Commission last week to provide emergency support to the sector. The measures will be put to a vote during Parliament's July plenary session. Within the current 2021-2027 CAP framework, Brussels has proposed establishing a temporary liquidity scheme, allowing member states to advance direct pay
items.titleIt: European Parliament activates urgent procedure for fertiliser price support measures

items.title: EU announces up to €540 million in aid for farmers and greater CAP flexibility
items.description: (ANSA) - ROME, JUN 25 - The European Commission has announced that up to €540 million from the CAP agricultural reserve could be mobilised to provide "targeted and exceptional" support to farmers affected by the conflict in the Middle East. EU member states are expected to approve the increase in available resources by the end of July. Brussels has also proposed a series of targeted CAP amendments, including a temporary liquidity scheme, greater flexibility to advance direct payments and the possibility to reprogramme funds to help farmers cope with rising fertiliser prices. Earlier this week,
items.titleIt: EU announces up to €540 million in aid for farmers and greater CAP flexibility
